title: SEGMENT 7 — Codeine, CYP2D6, and the children who died

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Codeine, CYP2D6, and the children who died
I want to tell you a story now that is, I think, the most haunting case in all of pharmacogenomics.
It's the story that, more than any other single case, drove regulatory action and made the field's case to skeptical clinicians who thought pharmacogenomics was an academic curiosity.
It's the story of codeine and CYP2D6 ultrarapid metabolizers.
And it involves dead babies.
Let me set up the pharmacology first.
Codeine is an opioid pain reliever.
It's been around for two centuries.
It's relatively mild — less potent than morphine, less abused historically, used for moderate pain and as a cough suppressant.
It was, until quite recently, considered safe enough to give to children.
Many of you have probably had it after a tooth extraction or for a bad cough.
Here's the thing about codeine that, for most of its history, almost nobody outside of pharmacology knew.
Codeine itself, the molecule you swallow, has almost no opioid activity.
It's not the drug.
It's a prodrug.
To produce its pain-relieving effect, codeine has to be converted in your liver to morphine — yes, morphine, the strong opioid — by an enzyme.
And that enzyme is CYP2D6.
About ten percent of the codeine you swallow gets converted to morphine in a normal metabolizer.
That's enough to give you mild pain relief.
Most of the rest gets converted to inactive metabolites and excreted.
Now apply the metabolizer phenotypes.
If you're a CYP2D6 poor metabolizer — about 7% of Europeans — you convert essentially no codeine to morphine.
Codeine doesn't work as a painkiller for you at all.
You'd be better off with acetaminophen.
This was actually known clinically for decades, in a vague way; some patients reported codeine just didn't help them.
Nobody knew why until the genetics was worked out.

If you're a CYP2D6 ultrarapid metabolizer — about 1% to 10% of Europeans depending on the population, and up to 30% in some North African populations — you convert codeine to morphine far more efficiently than normal.
We're not talking about a small uptick.
We're talking about producing morphine levels that, from a standard codeine dose, can be several times higher than expected.
Plasma morphine concentrations that, in some documented cases, have approached lethal levels.
And here is where the story turns dark.
In 2006, a case was published of a healthy newborn who died, in their first weeks of life, of morphine poisoning.
The baby was being breastfed.
The mother had been prescribed Tylenol with codeine for postpartum pain — a routine prescription, given to millions of women every year.
The mother was a CYP2D6 ultrarapid metabolizer.

She converted her codeine to morphine at an unusually high rate, the morphine concentrated in her breast milk, and the breastfeeding infant absorbed enough morphine to develop opioid toxicity and stop breathing.
The autopsy found morphine concentrations consistent with a fatal opioid overdose in a baby who had never been directly given an opioid.
This was the wakeup call.

Other cases were reported subsequently — infants who died after surgery from codeine given for post-tonsillectomy pain, where the infants themselves were ultrarapid metabolizers and converted their dose to dangerous morphine levels.
Children who stopped breathing after standard pediatric doses.
A pattern emerged that, in retrospect, had probably been killing children for decades but had been written off as unexplained deaths or sudden infant death syndrome.
In 2012 and 2013, the FDA issued a boxed warning on codeine and required label changes on tramadol — another opioid with similar CYP2D6 metabolism.

The recommendations: codeine is contraindicated in children under 12, contraindicated for post-tonsillectomy or post-adenoidectomy pain in children up to 18, and to be used with caution in breastfeeding mothers.
The European regulators issued parallel warnings.
This is the cautionary tale that, in my view, every clinician should know.

The ultrarapid metabolizer is dangerous, even from a standard dose, even of a drug that's been on the market safely for two hundred years, if the drug is a prodrug that gets converted to a more potent active form.

The fast chef converts the inactive ingredient into the active poison faster than the body can handle, and the patient downstream — sometimes a patient who's not even the one taking the drug, like a breastfeeding infant — pays the price.
The pharmacogenomics implication is direct.
Before you prescribe codeine — or tramadol, or any prodrug opioid metabolized by CYP2D6 — you should know the patient's CYP2D6 status, or you should default to a different drug.
For most clinical contexts now, this means we just don't use codeine in pediatrics anymore, and we're cautious with it in adults.
The risk of an undetected ultrarapid metabolizer is too high.
There's a beautiful, awful symmetry in the codeine story.
Poor metabolizers get no benefit from the drug; ultrarapid metabolizers get a near-overdose.
The "right" dose, in the middle, is calibrated for the average patient, and the people on the tails of the genetic distribution — both tails — are at risk in opposite directions.
Same drug, same dose, different chefs, different outcomes.
Sometimes the difference between "doesn't work" and "kills your baby.
This is why pharmacogenomics is not academic.
